children.name: Hydrochoerus
children.description: Capybaras — the world's largest rodents, semi-aquatic grazers of South American rivers, lakes, and marshes. A large male can weigh 66 kg. Capybaras are highly social, living in groups of 10–20 that may swell to hundreds during the dry season around shrinking water bodies. They are excellent swimmers and can remain submerged for up to five minutes to evade predators. Their eyes, ears, and nostrils are positioned high on the head, allowing them to monitor their surroundings while almost completely submerged. Jaguars, anacondas, caimans, pumas, and harpy eagles all prey on capybaras, making them a keystone prey species across South American wetlands.

children.name: Hydrochoerus hydrochaeris
children.description: The world's largest rodent — up to 1.3 m long and 66 kg. Highly social; their calm temperament and photogenic sociability has made them internet icons. In Venezuela they are farmed for meat under special Catholic dispensations that classify them as fish during Lent.

children.name: Hydrochoerus isthmius
children.description: A smaller relative of the capybara found in Panama, northwestern Colombia, and Venezuela, inhabiting marshes, rivers, and flooded forests near the isthmus. Reaches about 27 kg — roughly half the weight of the common capybara. Poorly studied and Data Deficient on the IUCN Red List; threatened by hunting and habitat loss.

children.name: Cavia
children.description: Guinea pigs — small, stout rodents of the Andes, domesticated by Andean peoples at least 5,000 years ago for food and still a dietary staple in Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador. Guinea pigs (called 'cuy') are more efficient at converting plant matter to protein than cattle, and they produce no greenhouse gases. Domestic guinea pigs (Cavia porcellus) descend from wild species still found in the Andes and differ significantly from their wild ancestors in coat colour, body size, and behaviour. Wild guinea pigs are shy and cryptic; domestic ones are placid and tolerant of handling.

children.name: Cavia porcellus
children.description: Domesticated for at least 5,000 years; no wild populations exist. A critical model organism in immunology and nutrition research — 'guinea pig' became synonymous with experimental subject. Highly vocal with a rich repertoire of purrs, rumbles, and squeaks.

children.name: Cavia aperea
children.description: The wild species most closely related to the domestic guinea pig. Lives in dense grassland and forest edge. Much more skittish than domestic animals, fleeing at the slightest disturbance.

children.name: Cavia tschudii
children.description: Found in the Andean highlands of Peru, Bolivia, and adjacent Chile and Argentina at elevations of 2,500–4,000 m. One of the likely ancestors of the domestic guinea pig alongside C. aperea. Lives in rocky grassland and shrubby slopes, sheltering in rock crevices and burrows. Named after Swiss naturalist Johann Jakob von Tschudi.

children.name: Cavia magna
children.description: The largest wild guinea pig, found in the Atlantic coastal lowlands of southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. Inhabits dense grassland and forest margins along watercourses. Much heavier than C. aperea; somewhat more tolerant of wet ground conditions.

children.name: Cavia fulgida
children.description: An Atlantic Forest species from southeastern Brazil, recognised by its glossier, more reddish-brown coat compared to other wild guinea pigs. Found in the mountain grasslands and forest clearings of the Serra da Mantiqueira and adjacent ranges. Less studied than its congeners.

children.name: Cavia intermedia
children.description: One of the world's most range-restricted rodents, Critically Endangered and known only from a single tiny island — Moleques do Sul — off Santa Catarina, Brazil, covering just 11 hectares. The entire wild population numbers only a few hundred individuals. Its extreme isolation and minuscule range make it acutely vulnerable to any single catastrophic event.

children.name: Cavia nana
children.description: The smallest member of the genus, found in the high-altitude grasslands of the Bolivian Andes above 3,000 m. Compact build and dense fur provide insulation against the cold puna climate. Little-studied in the wild; its precise range and ecology are poorly documented.

children.name: Dolichotis
children.description: Maras (Patagonian cavies) — large, long-legged guinea pig relatives that are among the most unusual rodents on Earth. With their long ears, hare-like legs, and swift bounding gait, they bear a striking resemblance to small deer or jackrabbits, a classic example of convergent evolution. Maras are unusual among rodents in forming long-term monogamous pairs; a male will follow his female closely and drive away rival males. They use a communal crèche system where multiple pairs deposit their young in a shared burrow tended by a rotating group of adults.

children.name: Dolichotis patagonum
children.description: Can reach 45 cm at the shoulder and weigh 9 kg — the fourth largest rodent. Runs at up to 45 km/h. Population declining due to livestock grazing, habitat loss, and hunting; near threatened.

children.name: Dolichotis salinicola
children.description: The smaller of the two mara species, found in the dry Chaco of Paraguay, Bolivia, and northwestern Argentina. More tolerant of arid conditions than the Patagonian Mara; inhabits thorn scrub and salt flats. Less studied than its larger relative; its social system and reproductive behaviour closely parallel those of D. patagonum.

children.name: Galea
children.description: Yellow-toothed cavies of South American grasslands and scrublands — small, fast-breeding rodents named for their distinctive yellowish enamel on the incisors. More slender and longer-legged than true guinea pigs (Cavia), they inhabit open and semi-open habitats from the altiplano to lowland Brazil. Highly social and diurnal, forming loose aggregations.

children.name: Galea musteloides
children.description: The most widespread of the yellow-toothed cavies, found from western Bolivia and Peru south through Argentina. Inhabits open puna grassland and Andean valleys. Diurnal and highly vocal; forms groups that forage together in short-grass habitat.

children.name: Galea spixii
children.description: Found in the caatinga dry scrubland of northeastern Brazil. Named after German naturalist Johann Baptist von Spix, who collected specimens in Brazil in the 1820s. Inhabits rocky caatinga and dry thorny scrub; often found sheltering under rock piles or in root tangles.

children.name: Galea flavidens
children.description: A Brazilian species of the cerrado savanna and gallery forest edges. Slightly larger than G. spixii with more strongly yellow-tinged incisors. Described from specimens collected by German naturalist Johann Friedrich von Brandt; biology poorly known.

children.name: Microcavia
children.description: Mountain cavies of the Andes and Patagonian steppe — small, sturdy guinea pig relatives adapted to rocky, arid, high-altitude habitats. They have relatively large eyes and ears and compact bodies. More agile than Cavia, they scramble over rocks and cliffs with ease. Diurnal and colonial; often found in small groups sunning on exposed boulders.

children.name: Microcavia australis
children.description: The most southerly cavy, ranging from Bolivia and northwestern Argentina south to Patagonia. Inhabits rocky slopes, brushy steppes, and the monte desert, sheltering in crevices and under shrubs. Remarkably cold-tolerant for a cavy, active year-round even in harsh Patagonian winters.

children.name: Microcavia niata
children.description: Found at high elevations (3,500–5,000 m) in the Bolivian and Peruvian Andes, one of the highest-altitude rodents in South America. 'Niata' refers to its slightly flattened face. Lives on rocky puna slopes with sparse vegetation, sheltering in rock crevices and burrows of other animals.

children.name: Microcavia shiptoni
children.description: Restricted to the upland valleys and rocky outcrops of Tucumán and Catamarca provinces in northwestern Argentina. Named in honour of a naturalist who collected specimens in the region. Data Deficient; rarely encountered and its ecology is virtually unstudied.

children.name: Kerodon
children.description: Rock cavies of northeastern Brazil — long-legged, almost tailless rodents adapted to life among granite outcrops (inselbergs) in the semi-arid caatinga. Unlike other cavies they can climb vertical rock faces and leap between boulders with remarkable agility. Closely related to capybaras despite their small size; dental formula differs from all other cavies.

children.name: Kerodon rupestris
children.description: An agile, cliff-dwelling rodent of the caatinga in northeastern Brazil, living among granite outcrops and boulders. Can climb vertical rock faces and jump 1 m between rocks. Feeds on leaves, bark, and grass; can survive without drinking water by extracting moisture from food. An important prey species for the rock python (Boa constrictor) and various raptors.
